species: Douglas Fir Crown in Gale -- sustained moan-roar
sound_type: Sustained moan-roar: (1) Aerodynamic: needle turbulence 1000-6000 Hz broadband (hiss); (2) Structural: trunk and branch flex, harmonics 20-200 Hz (deep creak and groan), gust cycle 5-15 sec; (3) Aeolian resonance: vortex shedding off cylindrical branches, 75-500 Hz (moan). Combined: 20-6000 Hz. Sustained at 70-85 dB at 20m. Duration: hours to days during gale events.
ecosystem: Exposed ridge, subalpine zone, Pacific Northwest Cascades and Olympics. Ridge crest wind speed 20-40% above valley (topographic acceleration). Gale threshold: 40+ mph sustained. Douglas fir at upper elevation limit: shorter, more exposed. Winter storm frequency at ridge crest: dozens of events per season.
season: Storm (peak winter and fall). Pacific Northwest winter storms produce gale conditions at exposed ridges on regular basis. Not a rare extreme -- a seasonal expectation.
milestone: THREE-MECHANISM MOAN-ROAR DOCUMENTED: aerodynamic (needle turbulence) + structural (trunk/branch flex) + Aeolian (vortex shedding tones). Douglas fir Pseudotsuga menziesii: 60-90m height, 2-4m diameter, 100-200 metric ton mass, sound speed in wood 5000 m/s. Aeolian frequency formula: f = (0.21 x wind speed) / branch diameter. Exposed ridge topographic acceleration 20-40%.

coordinates.description: Distributed: exposed ridge crests throughout the Cascades and Olympics in subalpine zone. Representative locations: Snoqualmie Pass ridges, North Cascades exposed crests, Olympic Peninsula high ridges.

acoustic_data.mechanism_1_aerodynamic: Needle turbulence at 40-50 mph gale. Turbulent wake behind needle fascicles (2-3 cm flat needles). Random broadband 1000-6000 Hz. The hiss component of the moan-roar. Dominant at highest wind speeds.
acoustic_data.mechanism_2_structural: Trunk fundamental resonance: 0.1-0.5 Hz (below audible). Audible harmonics: 20-200 Hz. Primary branch resonance: 2-20 Hz lowest modes + harmonics to 500 Hz. Gust cycle 5-15 sec creates rhythmic pulse in structural output. Deep creak and groan. The groan component.
acoustic_data.mechanism_3_aeolian: Vortex shedding off cylindrical branch elements. Strouhal number 0.21. Example: 5 cm branch at 40 mph (18 m/s) = 75 Hz. 2 cm branchlet at same speed = ~190 Hz. Pure tones emerge from broadband when wind is steady. The moan component -- tonal emergence from noise.
acoustic_data.combined: 20-6000 Hz. 70-85 dB at 20m from trunk. Sustained for duration of gale event (hours to days). Temporal structure: continuous broadband with rhythmic pulse (5-15 sec gust cycle) and superimposed Aeolian tones.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through sustained medium-high energy. Not impulsive (rock avalanche E=8) or hydraulically massive (tidal bore E=8). Sustained conifer acoustics at gale intensity. The same wind mechanism that contributes one layer to Hoh E=8 (degree 324); isolated to one tree on an exposed ridge = E=7.

nodes.title: Three-Mechanism Moan-Roar -- The Douglas Fir as Acoustic Instrument
nodes.description: The simultaneous operation of three acoustic mechanisms: aerodynamic needle turbulence (hiss, 1000-6000 Hz), structural trunk/branch flex (groan, 20-200 Hz), and Aeolian vortex shedding (moan, 75-500 Hz). The moan is the tonal component that distinguishes the Douglas fir gale from white-noise wind.

nodes.title: Aeolian Frequency -- Vortex Shedding Formula
nodes.description: f = (Strouhal number x wind speed) / cylinder diameter. For tree branches: Strouhal number ≈ 0.21. At 40 mph (18 m/s): 5 cm branch → 75 Hz; 2 cm branchlet → 190 Hz. The pure tonal component emerges from broadband noise when wind is steady and branch is sufficiently cylindrical.

nodes.title: Structural Resonance -- Wood Under Sustained Load
nodes.description: 150-metric-ton Douglas fir trunk under gale load: trunk fundamental 0.1-0.5 Hz (felt, not heard). Audible harmonics 20-200 Hz from trunk; 2-500 Hz from branches. Gust cycle 5-15 sec creates rhythmic pulse. Sound speed in wood: 5000 m/s (vs. 343 m/s in air).

cross_references.relationship: Different crown morphology. Two conifer wind acoustic profiles documented.
General. Degree 326 is gale-specific, exposed-ridge, three-mechanism analysis.
Hoh wind layer is one of four. Degree 326 is the same mechanism on a single ridge tree without the other three layers.
Next degree: above the trees. No crown acoustics. Pure atmospheric wind. The treeline is the acoustic boundary.
E=6 + E=7 = E=13. Compositional precision under sustained load vs. structural precision under sustained gale force.
